seo.title: CSS Flexbox Complete Guide - display: flex, justify-content, align-items, gap, flex-wrap | Modern Age Coders
seo.description: Master CSS Flexbox. Learn display: flex, flex-direction, justify-content, align-items, flex-wrap, gap, flex-grow, flex-shrink, flex-basis, order, align-self. Build responsive navbars, card grids, pricing tables, and perfectly centered layouts. 62+ practice questions.
seo.keywords: css flexbox, display flex, justify-content, align-items, flex-direction, flex-wrap, gap css, flex-grow, flex-basis, responsive navbar flexbox, center div flexbox, flex shorthand

content.what:
What Is Flexbox?

Flexbox (Flexible Box Layout) is a CSS layout system designed to arrange items in a single row or column. With one line -- display: flex -- a container's direct children become flex items that you can align, distribute, wrap, and order with a handful of intuitive properties.

Before flexbox, centering a div was a running joke. With flexbox, it is two lines. Navbars, card grids, pricing tables, form layouts, sidebar + content layouts -- all become dramatically simpler. Flexbox is arguably the single most important CSS feature of the last 15 years.

.container {
  display: flex;
  justify-content: center;  /* horizontal centering */
  align-items: center;      /* vertical centering */
}
/* Any children are now perfectly centered. Done. */
content.why:
Why Is Flexbox So Important?

1. It Solves Layout Problems That Used to Be Painful

Centering a div was hard. Equal-height columns were hard. Spacing items evenly was hard. Building a responsive navbar was hard. Flexbox makes all of these trivial. A few properties replace hundreds of lines of float hacks and table tricks.

2. Every Modern Website Uses It

Open any modern website in DevTools. You will see display: flex everywhere -- navbars, buttons, form groups, card layouts, section headers. Flexbox is the default layout tool for one-dimensional arrangements.

3. It Is Responsive by Default

Flex items automatically adjust to available space. Add flex-wrap: wrap and they flow onto new rows when the container gets narrow. Combined with flex: 1, you get responsive layouts with almost no effort.

4. It Makes CSS Fun Again

Once you understand flexbox, layout becomes intuitive. You stop fighting CSS and start actually designing. This is the chapter where CSS clicks for most learners.
content.explanation:
Detailed Explanation

1. Flex Container vs Flex Items

Apply display: flex to a PARENT element. The parent becomes a flex container. Its DIRECT children become flex items. Flexbox properties are split into two groups:

  • Container properties: flex-direction, justify-content, align-items, flex-wrap, gap, align-content
  • Item properties: flex-grow, flex-shrink, flex-basis, flex, order, align-self

2. Main Axis vs Cross Axis

This is the most important concept. Flexbox has two axes:

  • Main axis: the direction items are laid out in (horizontal by default)
  • Cross axis: perpendicular to the main axis (vertical by default)

When you change flex-direction, the axes rotate. In a row, the main axis is horizontal. In a column, the main axis is vertical. This matters because justify-content aligns along the MAIN axis and align-items aligns along the CROSS axis.

3. flex-direction

.container {
  display: flex;
  flex-direction: row;            /* default: left to right */
  flex-direction: row-reverse;    /* right to left */
  flex-direction: column;         /* top to bottom */
  flex-direction: column-reverse; /* bottom to top */
}

4. justify-content (MAIN AXIS)

Distributes items along the main axis.

justify-content: flex-start;    /* default: packed at start */
justify-content: flex-end;      /* packed at end */
justify-content: center;        /* centered */
justify-content: space-between; /* first at start, last at end, equal space between */
justify-content: space-around;  /* equal space around each item */
justify-content: space-evenly;  /* equal space between AND at edges */

Memorize space-between for navbars (logo left, links right) and center for everything else.

5. align-items (CROSS AXIS)

Aligns items perpendicular to the main axis.

align-items: stretch;     /* default: fill cross axis */
align-items: flex-start;  /* top (in a row) */
align-items: flex-end;    /* bottom (in a row) */
align-items: center;      /* vertically centered (in a row) */
align-items: baseline;    /* aligned to text baselines */

6. The Holy Grail: Centering

.center {
  display: flex;
  justify-content: center;
  align-items: center;
  /* Anything inside is perfectly centered -- horizontally AND vertically */
}

This one pattern solves the historical 'center a div' problem. Remember it.

7. flex-wrap

By default, flex items try to fit on a single line, even if they have to shrink. flex-wrap lets them wrap onto multiple lines.

flex-wrap: nowrap;   /* default: all on one line */
flex-wrap: wrap;     /* wrap to new lines when needed */
flex-wrap: wrap-reverse;

8. gap

The modern replacement for margin between items. gap works inside flex and grid containers and is much simpler than setting margins.

.container {
  display: flex;
  gap: 16px;          /* space between items */
  gap: 16px 24px;     /* row-gap | column-gap */
}

Use gap. Stop using margins on flex items. Trust me.

9. align-content

Only applies when flex-wrap causes multiple lines. Controls how those LINES are distributed along the cross axis.

align-content: flex-start;
align-content: center;
align-content: space-between;
align-content: space-around;

10. Flex Item Properties

flex-grow

How much an item grows to fill extra space. Default is 0 (do not grow). Values are ratios: flex-grow: 2 grows twice as fast as flex-grow: 1.

.main    { flex-grow: 1; }  /* takes any extra space */
.sidebar { flex-grow: 0; }  /* keeps its natural size */

flex-shrink

How much an item shrinks when there is not enough space. Default is 1 (can shrink). Set to 0 to prevent shrinking.

.logo { flex-shrink: 0; }  /* never shrinks */

flex-basis

The initial size of the item before grow/shrink is applied.

.item { flex-basis: 200px; }  /* starts at 200px, then grows or shrinks */

flex Shorthand

Sets grow, shrink, and basis in one line. The common values you will actually use:

flex: 1;        /* = flex: 1 1 0 -- grow to fill, shrink if needed, start at 0 */
flex: 0 0 auto; /* no grow, no shrink, use content size */
flex: 1 0 200px; /* grow, no shrink, start at 200px */
flex: none;     /* no grow, no shrink, use content size */

Most of the time you just use flex: 1 on items that should fill available space.

order

Reorder items visually without changing the HTML.

.first { order: 1; }
.second { order: 2; }
.hero { order: -1; } /* shows first regardless of source order */

align-self

Override align-items for a single flex item.

.special { align-self: flex-end; }  /* aligned to bottom while others center */

11. Common Flexbox Patterns

Centering everything

display: flex;
justify-content: center;
align-items: center;

Navbar (logo left, links right)

display: flex;
justify-content: space-between;
align-items: center;

Equal-width card grid

.grid { display: flex; gap: 20px; }
.card { flex: 1; }

Sticky footer

body {
  min-height: 100vh;
  display: flex;
  flex-direction: column;
}
main { flex: 1; }

codeExamples.title: Perfect Centering (The Holy Grail)
codeExamples.code:
Hi, I'm Aarav

Perfectly centered horizontally and vertically with flexbox.
codeExamples.explanation: Three lines of flexbox replace the decade-long struggle of centering an element. display: flex makes the container a flex container. justify-content: center centers along the main axis (horizontal). align-items: center centers along the cross axis (vertical).
codeExamples.output: A full-screen gradient hero with a heading and tagline perfectly centered both horizontally and vertically.

codeExamples.title: Responsive Navbar
codeExamples.code:
Priya.dev
  • Home
  • Blog
  • Projects
  • About

Welcome, friend!

This page has a flexbox navbar: logo left, links center, button right.
codeExamples.explanation: The nav uses justify-content: space-between to push the logo left, button right, with the links in between. The links themselves are a nested flex container with gap: 24px for even horizontal spacing. This is the standard navbar pattern.
codeExamples.output: A modern navbar with a purple logo on the left, navigation links in the middle, and a purple sign-up button on the right.

codeExamples.title: Equal-Width Card Grid
codeExamples.code:

Projects

Build real apps you can show off on your portfolio.

Flexible

Learn at your own pace with recorded backup classes.

Community

Meet other kids who love coding. By Kavya.
codeExamples.explanation: flex: 1 1 240px means each card grows (1), shrinks (1), and has a base width of 240px. With flex-wrap: wrap, cards reflow onto new rows when the container gets narrower. Resize the window to see the responsive behavior.
codeExamples.output: A responsive grid of 4 feature cards that flex to equal widths on wide screens and wrap to multiple rows on narrow screens.

codeExamples.explanation: The outer container uses display: flex with gap and wrap. Each plan is also a flex container with flex-direction: column so the button can be pushed to the bottom using margin-top: auto. This ensures buttons line up even if feature lists have different lengths.
codeExamples.output: Three pricing cards side by side. The middle one is highlighted and slightly larger. All buttons align to the bottom regardless of feature list length.

codeExamples.title: Sticky Footer Layout
codeExamples.code:
Ishaan.dev
v1.0

Short Page

Even with very little content, the footer stays at the bottom of the viewport because of flexbox.

This works by making body a flex column that is at least 100vh tall. The main element grows (flex: 1) to fill all leftover space, pushing the footer down.

Made with flexbox by Ishaan - 2026
codeExamples.explanation: The body is a flex column with min-height: 100vh. The main element has flex: 1, meaning it grows to fill any leftover vertical space. The footer is pushed to the bottom even when the page has minimal content. This replaces the old sticky footer hacks.
codeExamples.output: A page with a header, short main content, and a footer that is always pinned to the bottom of the viewport even with little content.

codeExamples.title: Order and align-self
codeExamples.code:
order: rearrange visually without changing HTML. Cyan is first, amber is last.

A
B
C
D
E

align-self: override align-items on individual items

Top
Mid
Tall
Bot
Mid
codeExamples.explanation: order lets you visually reorder flex items without touching the HTML. Items with lower order come first (default is 0). align-self overrides align-items for individual flex items -- the Top item goes to the top of the container while others stay centered.
codeExamples.output: Two rows of colored boxes. The first shows reordering (cyan first, amber last), the second shows individual items aligned to the top, center, and bottom.

commonMistakes.title: Applying Flex Properties to the Wrong Element
commonMistakes.error: display: flex on an item does not move the item itself. It only affects the item's children.
commonMistakes.explanation: display: flex is always applied to the parent. The parent's children are the flex items that get aligned. Put display: flex on the thing containing what you want to align.

commonMistakes.title: Confusing justify-content and align-items
commonMistakes.error: In a row, justify-content is horizontal and align-items is vertical. In a column, they SWAP -- justify-content becomes vertical and align-items becomes horizontal. This trips up beginners.
commonMistakes.correctCode:
/* Always think: justify-content = MAIN axis, align-items = CROSS axis */
/* The axes rotate with flex-direction. */
commonMistakes.explanation: Never memorize 'justify = horizontal'. Memorize 'justify = main axis'. Then remember: row main axis is horizontal, column main axis is vertical.

commonMistakes.title: Using Margins Instead of gap
commonMistakes.error: Margins on flex items create uneven spacing at the edges and require last-child hacks.
commonMistakes.explanation: gap is supported in all modern browsers. It only adds space BETWEEN items (not at the edges) and requires no hacks. Always use gap for spacing flex items.

commonMistakes.title: Forgetting flex-wrap on Responsive Layouts
commonMistakes.error: Without flex-wrap, items try to squeeze onto a single line, shrinking past their flex-basis.
commonMistakes.explanation: Add flex-wrap: wrap whenever you want items to flow onto new lines on smaller screens. It is essential for responsive card grids.

summary: display: flex makes an element a flex container. Its direct children become flex items that can be aligned, distributed, wrapped, and ordered with a few properties.
Flexbox has a MAIN axis (direction of flow, horizontal by default) and a CROSS axis (perpendicular). flex-direction changes which axis is which.
justify-content aligns items along the MAIN axis. Values: flex-start, flex-end, center, space-between (for navbars), space-around, space-evenly.
align-items aligns items along the CROSS axis. Values: stretch (default), flex-start, flex-end, center (for vertical centering), baseline.
The holy grail of centering: display: flex; justify-content: center; align-items: center. Centers any child both horizontally and vertically.
flex-wrap: wrap lets items flow onto multiple lines when they do not fit. Essential for responsive card grids.
gap is the modern way to space flex items. Works inside the container, adds space only between items (not at edges), and is far simpler than using margins.
flex-grow, flex-shrink, and flex-basis control how items size. The flex shorthand combines them. flex: 1 is the most common value -- it means grow to fill available space.
order lets you visually rearrange items without changing the HTML. Useful for responsive layouts where the visual order differs from the source order.
Common patterns to memorize: centering (justify + align center), navbar (space-between), card grid (flex: 1 + flex-wrap), sticky footer (flex column + flex: 1 on main).

topicSpecific.question: What does display: flex do?
topicSpecific.answer: Makes the element a flex container. Its direct children become flex items that can be aligned and distributed.
topicSpecific.explanation: display: flex on a parent turns it into a flex container. Only direct children become flex items -- grandchildren are not affected unless they are themselves in another flex container.

topicSpecific.question: What axis does justify-content align along?
topicSpecific.hint: It is the primary flow direction.
topicSpecific.answer: The MAIN axis (horizontal in a row, vertical in a column).
topicSpecific.explanation: justify-content always aligns along the main axis. Because the axes rotate with flex-direction, it aligns horizontally in a row and vertically in a column.

topicSpecific.question: What axis does align-items align along?
topicSpecific.answer: The CROSS axis (perpendicular to main).
topicSpecific.explanation: align-items aligns along the cross axis. In a row, cross axis is vertical. In a column, cross axis is horizontal.

topicSpecific.answer: Centers child content both horizontally and vertically inside the container.
topicSpecific.explanation: This is the holy grail of centering. Three lines replace the decade-long struggle of centering a div.

topicSpecific.question: What is the default flex-direction?
topicSpecific.answer: row
topicSpecific.explanation: By default, flex items are laid out in a row (horizontal, left to right in LTR languages).

topicSpecific.question: What does justify-content: space-between do?
topicSpecific.answer: First item at the start, last item at the end, with equal space distributed between items.
topicSpecific.explanation: space-between is perfect for navbars: logo on the left, links/button on the right, with no space on the outside.

topicSpecific.question: What does gap: 16px do in a flex container?
topicSpecific.answer: Adds 16px of space between all flex items (but not at the outer edges of the container).
topicSpecific.explanation: gap is the modern, clean way to space flex items. It avoids the need for margins with last-child hacks.

topicSpecific.question: What does flex-wrap: wrap do?
topicSpecific.answer: Allows flex items to wrap onto new lines when they do not fit on one line.
topicSpecific.explanation: Without flex-wrap, items try to squeeze onto a single line. With wrap, they flow to new lines when the container is too narrow.

topicSpecific.question: What does flex: 1 do?
topicSpecific.answer: Makes the item grow to fill any available space (equivalent to flex: 1 1 0).
topicSpecific.explanation: flex: 1 is the most common flex shorthand. It tells the item to share leftover space equally with other flex: 1 items.

topicSpecific.question: Where does display: flex go to align a div inside a container?
topicSpecific.answer: On the PARENT (the container), not on the child. The parent becomes the flex container; the child becomes a flex item.
topicSpecific.explanation: Flex properties on a child make it a flex container for ITS children. To align a child, apply flex on its parent.

topicSpecific.question:
What does this navbar pattern do?
.nav { display: flex; justify-content: space-between; align-items: center; }
topicSpecific.hint: Think navbar with logo and menu.
topicSpecific.answer: Places children at opposite ends (logo left, menu right), with all items vertically centered.
topicSpecific.explanation: The canonical navbar pattern. space-between pushes the first child left and the last child right; align-items: center vertically centers them.

topicSpecific.question: What does flex-direction: column change?
topicSpecific.answer: Flex items stack vertically. The main axis becomes vertical and the cross axis becomes horizontal.
topicSpecific.explanation: With column direction, justify-content aligns vertically and align-items aligns horizontally. The meanings swap.

topicSpecific.question:
What happens?
.grid { display: flex; }
.card { flex: 1; }
topicSpecific.answer: Each card grows to take an equal share of the available space in the row.
topicSpecific.explanation: flex: 1 on every item means they all share leftover space equally. Four cards would each take 25% of the width.

topicSpecific.question: What does align-self: flex-end do on a flex item?
topicSpecific.answer: Aligns that specific item to the end of the cross axis, overriding the container's align-items value.
topicSpecific.explanation: align-self is applied to individual items and overrides the container's align-items just for that one item.

topicSpecific.question: What is the difference between justify-content: space-between and space-around?
topicSpecific.answer: space-between puts no space at the outer edges; space-around adds equal space around each item (so outer edges get half the space between items).
topicSpecific.explanation: space-between: edges flush. space-around: equal space around every item, half-gaps at edges. space-evenly: equal gaps including edges.

topicSpecific.question: What does flex: 1 1 200px mean?
topicSpecific.answer: flex-grow: 1 (grow to fill), flex-shrink: 1 (can shrink), flex-basis: 200px (start at 200px).
topicSpecific.explanation: The flex shorthand is in the order grow shrink basis. The item starts at 200px, grows if there is space, and shrinks if there is not.

topicSpecific.question: How do you prevent an item from shrinking?
topicSpecific.answer: flex-shrink: 0 or flex: 0 0 auto
topicSpecific.explanation: A flex-shrink of 0 means the item never shrinks below its base size. Useful for logos and icons that should keep their size.

topicSpecific.question:
What does this sticky footer code do?
body { min-height: 100vh; display: flex; flex-direction: column; }
main { flex: 1; }
topicSpecific.hint: Main grows to push footer down.
topicSpecific.answer: Makes the footer stick to the bottom of the viewport even when main content is short. main grows to fill all leftover vertical space.
topicSpecific.explanation: body is at least 100vh tall and stacks children in a column. main has flex: 1, so it expands to take all remaining vertical space, pushing the footer to the bottom.

topicSpecific.question: What does order: -1 do?
topicSpecific.answer: Places the item before all other items with the default order (0) regardless of source order in HTML.
topicSpecific.explanation: order lets you visually rearrange flex items. Default is 0. Negative values come before 0, positive values come after.

topicSpecific.question: What is the default align-items value?
topicSpecific.answer: stretch
topicSpecific.explanation: By default, flex items stretch to fill the cross axis of the container. Setting a specific height on the item or changing align-items turns this off.

topicSpecific.question:
What is the layout?
.container { display: flex; flex-direction: column; height: 500px; justify-content: space-between; }
/* Three child divs */
topicSpecific.answer: Three children stacked vertically: first at the top, last at the bottom, middle evenly spaced between them.
topicSpecific.explanation: In column direction, justify-content aligns vertically. space-between pushes first to top and last to bottom, distributing the middle child in the leftover vertical space.

topicSpecific.answer: All 6 cards try to fit on one line, shrinking below 300px. Without flex-wrap, they squeeze instead of wrapping to a new row.
topicSpecific.explanation: By default flex-wrap is nowrap. Add flex-wrap: wrap so items can flow to new lines when there is not enough space.

topicSpecific.answer: .a takes 2x the space of .b and .c. Total is 4 parts: .a gets 50%, .b gets 25%, .c gets 25% of the available space.
topicSpecific.explanation: flex-grow values are ratios. Total ratio = 2+1+1 = 4. Each item takes its proportional share: 2/4, 1/4, 1/4.

topicSpecific.question: When should you use flex-direction: column?
topicSpecific.answer: Use column when you want items stacked vertically: sidebars, forms, card contents, footers, anything where children should flow top to bottom. Remember that in column mode, justify-content controls vertical alignment and align-items controls horizontal.
topicSpecific.explanation: Column direction is common inside individual components (cards, forms, sidebars). Row direction is common for layouts with side-by-side items.

topicSpecific.question: Why is gap better than margin for spacing flex items?
topicSpecific.answer: gap only adds space between items, not at the outer edges, so you do not need hacks like :last-child { margin: 0 }. It is simpler, cleaner, and works consistently in both flex and grid layouts.
topicSpecific.explanation: gap also automatically handles wrapping: when items wrap to a new line, the gap is preserved between rows. Margins do not handle this as elegantly.

mixed.question: Does display: flex go on the parent or child?
mixed.answer: The parent (container).
mixed.explanation: display: flex is applied to the container. Its direct children automatically become flex items.

mixed.question: Which property wraps items onto new lines?
mixed.answer: flex-wrap: wrap
mixed.explanation: Default is nowrap. Set flex-wrap to wrap so items flow onto multiple lines when the container is narrow.

mixed.question: What centers items horizontally in a row?
mixed.answer: justify-content: center
mixed.explanation: In a row (default flex-direction), the main axis is horizontal. justify-content: center centers along the main axis.

mixed.question: What centers items vertically in a row?
mixed.answer: align-items: center
mixed.explanation: In a row, the cross axis is vertical. align-items aligns along the cross axis.

mixed.question: What is the flex shorthand default?
mixed.answer: flex: 0 1 auto (no grow, can shrink, based on content).
mixed.explanation: By default, flex items do not grow to fill space, can shrink if needed, and size based on their content.

mixed.question: Which property puts gaps between flex items?
mixed.answer: gap
mixed.explanation: gap is the modern property for spacing flex items. It supports gap: 16px (uniform) or gap: 16px 24px (row-gap | column-gap).

mixed.question: What does flex-direction: column-reverse do?
mixed.answer: Stacks items vertically from bottom to top (reverses the source order visually).
mixed.explanation: column-reverse stacks the items vertically but with the first item at the bottom and the last at the top.

mixed.question: What is the difference between space-between, space-around, and space-evenly?
mixed.answer: space-between: no space at edges, equal between items. space-around: equal space around each item (half-gaps at edges). space-evenly: equal space everywhere including edges.
mixed.explanation: Use space-between for navbars. Use space-evenly when you want equal margins all around items, including the container edges.

mixed.question: How do you make a flex item NOT shrink?
mixed.answer: flex-shrink: 0
mixed.explanation: flex-shrink: 0 disables shrinking. The item keeps its flex-basis size even if there is not enough space.

mixed.question: What does order: 2 do compared to another item with order: 1?
mixed.answer: The order: 2 item is placed after the order: 1 item (higher order = later).
mixed.explanation: Flex items are laid out in order of their order value. Lower comes first. Default order is 0.

mixed.question: What is the difference between align-items and align-content?
mixed.answer: align-items aligns items within each line along the cross axis. align-content aligns whole lines in a wrapped multi-line flex container along the cross axis. align-content only has an effect when there is more than one line.
mixed.explanation: With flex-wrap creating multiple rows of items, align-content distributes those rows. align-items distributes items within each row.

mixed.question: How do you center a single item perfectly with flexbox?
mixed.answer: Apply to the parent: display: flex; justify-content: center; align-items: center;
mixed.explanation: This centers the child both horizontally and vertically with three lines of CSS. Works regardless of the child's size.

mixed.question: What is a common flexbox navbar pattern?
mixed.answer: display: flex; justify-content: space-between; align-items: center. This places the first child (logo) at the left and the last child at the right, with everything vertically centered.
mixed.explanation: This is the standard navbar recipe. It works for any number of children, placing the first at the start and the last at the end.

mixed.question: How do you build a 3-column card grid that wraps on small screens?
mixed.answer: .grid { display: flex; flex-wrap: wrap; gap: 20px; } .card { flex: 1 1 280px; }
mixed.explanation: flex: 1 1 280px means each card grows, shrinks, and has a minimum preferred width of 280px. flex-wrap: wrap lets them flow to new rows on narrow screens.

mixed.question: Why does main content with flex: 1 push the footer to the bottom?
mixed.answer: When body is a flex column with min-height: 100vh, the items stack vertically. Main with flex: 1 grows to fill all leftover vertical space, pushing the footer against the bottom of the viewport.
mixed.explanation: The flex-grow value of 1 tells main to consume all available vertical space. Header and footer keep their natural sizes.

mixed.question:
What happens?
.container { display: flex; height: 200px; }
.item { align-self: center; }
mixed.answer: The item is vertically centered in the 200px container. align-self overrides align-items even if align-items was not explicitly set.
mixed.explanation: align-self works independently on each item. The default cross-axis alignment is stretch, but align-self: center overrides it for this specific item.

mixed.answer: .a gets 1/6, .b gets 2/6 (1/3), .c gets 3/6 (1/2) of the available space.
mixed.explanation: Total flex-grow = 6. Each item takes its proportional share. Useful for three-column layouts with different widths.

mixed.question: What does flex-basis do that width does not?
mixed.hint: Flex basis is the starting point for flex calculations.
mixed.answer: flex-basis is the starting size used by the flex algorithm before grow/shrink is applied. In a flex container, flex-basis usually wins over width for flex sizing calculations. It can also use the 'auto' keyword (use content size) or 0 (start from zero and grow).
mixed.explanation: In practice, in modern code you typically set flex-basis via the flex shorthand: flex: 1 1 200px.

mixed.question: What pattern pushes one flex item all the way to the right?
mixed.answer: Apply margin-left: auto to the item you want to push right. Auto margins consume all available space, sending the item to the opposite edge.
mixed.explanation: This is a classic trick: in a row of items with, say, Logo, Link1, Link2, Button, you can set margin-left: auto on Button to push just it to the far right, keeping the others grouped on the left.

mixed.question: Why is flexbox easier than float-based layouts?
mixed.answer: Flexbox handles alignment, spacing, ordering, and wrapping with dedicated properties. It does not need clearfix hacks. It gives equal-height columns for free. It handles centering easily. Floats were never designed for layout -- they were designed for text wrapping around images.
mixed.explanation: Floats required many hacks (clearfix, faux columns, equal-height tricks). Flexbox was designed from scratch for modern layouts.

mcqs.question: How do you make an element a flex container?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. display: flex turns an element into a flex container. Its direct children become flex items.

mcqs.question: Which property aligns items along the main axis?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. justify-content distributes items along the main axis.

mcqs.question: Which property aligns items along the cross axis?
mcqs.explanation: A is correct. align-items aligns along the cross axis (perpendicular to flex-direction).

mcqs.question: What is the default flex-direction?
mcqs.explanation: A is correct. Default is row: items laid out horizontally left to right.

mcqs.question: What does flex: 1 do?

options.B: Makes the item grow to fill available space

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. flex: 1 (shorthand for flex: 1 1 0) makes the item grow to share available space.

mcqs.question: Which value pushes the first and last items to opposite edges?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. space-between puts first at start, last at end, with equal space between them.

mcqs.question: Which lets items wrap onto new lines?
mcqs.explanation: A is correct. flex-wrap: wrap allows items to flow onto multiple lines.

mcqs.question: Which is the modern replacement for margins between flex items?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. gap creates space between items without adding margin at the edges. Works in flex and grid containers.

mcqs.question: How do you center a div both horizontally and vertically with flexbox?

options.B: display: flex; justify-content: center; align-items: center

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. The holy grail centering pattern: three lines, perfectly centered.

mcqs.question: Which property overrides align-items for a single item?
mcqs.explanation: A is correct. align-self is applied to individual flex items to override the container's align-items for that item.

mcqs.question: In flex-direction: column, what does justify-content control?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. justify-content always aligns along the main axis. In column direction, main axis is vertical, so justify-content aligns vertically.

mcqs.question: In flex-direction: column, what does align-items control?
mcqs.explanation: A is correct. align-items aligns along the cross axis. In column direction, cross axis is horizontal.

mcqs.question: What does flex: 0 0 auto mean?

options.B: No grow, no shrink, content-based size

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. flex: 0 0 auto means flex-grow: 0, flex-shrink: 0, flex-basis: auto -- the item stays at its content size and never grows or shrinks.

mcqs.question: What is the classic navbar flex pattern?

options.B: display: flex; justify-content: space-between; align-items: center;

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. space-between pushes logo left and menu right; align-items: center vertically centers them.

mcqs.question: How do you make items stack vertically?
mcqs.explanation: A is correct. flex-direction: column stacks items vertically, making the main axis vertical.

mcqs.question: Which flex value makes an item twice as wide as others with flex: 1?
mcqs.explanation: A is correct. flex values are ratios. flex: 2 grows twice as much as flex: 1 from the remaining space.

mcqs.question: Which creates a responsive card grid that wraps on narrow screens?

options.A: display: flex; flex-wrap: wrap; gap: 20px; then .card { flex: 1 1 260px; }

mcqs.explanation: A is correct. flex: 1 1 260px gives each card a flexible size that grows but never shrinks below 260px, and flex-wrap lets them wrap.

mcqs.question: What is align-content used for?

options.A: Alignment of text inside an item
options.B: Alignment of multiple lines in a wrapped flex container along the cross axis

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. align-content only matters when flex-wrap creates multiple rows. It distributes those rows along the cross axis.

mcqs.question: How do you build a sticky footer with flexbox?

options.B: body { min-height: 100vh; display: flex; flex-direction: column; } main { flex: 1; }

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Flex column body, main grows to fill leftover space, pushing footer down. Works even with short content.

mcqs.question: What does order: -1 do?

options.B: Moves the item to appear before items with default order

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Lower order values come first. -1 is less than the default (0), so the item appears before all others.

mcqs.question: Why might flex-wrap be needed for a responsive grid?

options.B: Without it, items squeeze onto one line instead of wrapping to new rows

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Default is nowrap. Items shrink below their flex-basis instead of wrapping. flex-wrap: wrap is required for responsive layouts.

mcqs.question: Which combination grows items equally but respects a minimum size?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. 1 1 280px means grow, shrink, start at 280px. Items grow equally but start from a 280px minimum, ideal for card grids.

mcqs.question: How do you push ONE flex item to the far right while others stay on the left?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. margin-left: auto consumes all available space to the left of the item, shoving it to the right edge.

mcqs.question: Why is flexbox better than floats for layouts?

options.B: Flexbox handles alignment, equal heights, ordering, and wrapping natively without hacks
options.D: Floats do not work on mobile

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Floats required clearfix hacks and complex math. Flexbox was designed for layout and handles everything cleanly.

mcqs.question: What happens when you combine flex: 1 on 3 items but also set flex-shrink: 0 on one of them?

options.B: The shrink-0 item keeps its size while the others shrink

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. When space is tight, the shrink-0 item stays at its base size. The other items (with shrink > 0) absorb the compression.
